did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach, specifically a pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (VGG16) with transfer learning, to classify skin cancer lesions. The model was trained on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets to distinguish between various dermatological conditions (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, nevus) using both dermoscopic and macroscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a generalized deep learning model using transfer learning (VGG16) on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. It reports specific performance metrics (ROC AUC of 0.97, accuracy of 79%) and describes a real-time application test using a web-camera stream. Although published in a conference proceedings (CoDIT 2025), it is a full research article detailing methodology, results, and conclusions, not a review or abstract.

title: Generalised Skin Cancer Detection using Transfer Learning for Real-world Scenarios
journal: 2025 11th International Conference on Control, Decision and Information Technologies (CoDIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the limitations of single-image skin cancer diagnosis by developing a generalized deep learning model that integrates both dermoscopic and macroscopic image datasets. The researchers utilized transfer learning with a pre-trained VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network, which was fine-tuned on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets after addressing class imbalances via random over-sampling. The model achieved an accuracy of 79%, an F1 score of 79%, and a Receiver Operating Characteristic Area Under the Curve (ROC AUC) score of 0.97. To validate real-world applicability, the system was tested in a live environment using a web-camera stream for real-time lesion classification. The results demonstrate strong potential for scalable and accessible AI-driven skin cancer diagnosis in settings lacking specialized dermatoscopic equipment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified in both HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets; merged with 'bcc' from HAM10000 for the combined model.

paper_terms: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease
lesion_types_studied.details: Included Bowen's Disease (BOD) as in-situ SCC; merged 'akiec' from HAM10000 and 'SCC'/'ACK' from PAD-UFES-20 under the class 'akiec'.
Merged 'mel' from HAM10000 and 'MEL' from PAD-UFES-20 under the class 'mel'.
Merged 'nv' from HAM10000 and 'NEV' from PAD-UFES-20 under the class 'nv'; noted as the most dominant class in HAM10000.
Merged 'bkl' from HAM10000 and 'SEK' from PAD-UFES-20 under the class 'bkl'.
Included in PAD-UFES-20; merged with SCC and ACK under the class 'akiec' for the combined model.

all_task_types.target: Discrete dermatological lesion classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes the task as 'skin cancer classification' and 'classifying skin lesions'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a VGG16 transfer learning architecture with custom fully connected layers. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, and ROC AUC, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The model distinguishes between multiple specific lesion types (multiclass classification).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from diverse populations and acquired using various modalities.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease (akiec)

per_class.nv: Most dominant class (exact count not reported)

use.training: Combined with PAD-UFES-20, preprocessed (resized to 64x64, normalized), and used for training the VGG16 model after addressing class imbalance via Random Over-Sampling.
use.other: Pre-trained on ImageNet; fine-tuned on the combined dataset.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A skin lesion dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones, containing 2,298 samples of six types of skin lesions with clinical features.

use.training: Combined with HAM10000, preprocessed (resized to 64x64, normalized), and used for training the VGG16 model after addressing class imbalance via Random Over-Sampling.
use.other: Pre-trained on ImageNet; fine-tuned on the combined dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

Combined HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20.notes: The model achieved an accuracy of 79% and an F1 score of 79%. The ROC AUC score was 0.97. Per-class F1 scores for cancerous lesions were: Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc) 0.72, Melanoma (mel) 0.68, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (akiec) 0.76. Benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl) had an F1 score of 0.69. The model classified vascular lesions and dermatofibroma with the highest precision. The model most confused 'bcc' samples with 'akiec' samples, and 'nv' was confused with 'mel' 101 times. The paper also reports a ROC AUC of 0.96 in the conclusion section.

VGG16 (Transfer Learning).Performance: Preliminary results showed the ability to detect and classify lesions, but accuracy was impacted by inconsistent lighting and varying camera focal points.

Real-time Web-camera Application.notes: The model was tested in a live environment using a web-camera stream. While it demonstrated the capacity to leverage pre-trained networks, inconsistent lighting and varying camera focal points obscured or distorted essential features, resulting in potential misclassification or false negatives. The system utilized OpenCV for skin detection, contour detection, and ROI processing.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset; base model frozen (include_top=False, base_model.trainable = False) to act as a fixed feature extractor.

architecture_details.base_model: VGG16 (16 layers including convolutional, max-pooling, and fully connected layers; only convolutional layers used for feature extraction)

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Random Over-Sampling (minority classes duplicated) and undersampling of dominant 'nv' class to 3000 samples

classification_or_output_head.units: Number of classes (7: bcc, akiec, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ensemble Deep Learning model (combining VGG, CapsNet, and ResNet)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform dimension of 64x64 pixels using OpenCV's cv2.resize function to ensure consistent input sizes for the model.
Data normalization (scaling pixel intensity values) to standardize brightness and contrast, aligning pixel values to a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one.
Random Over-Sampling to address class imbalances in the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets.
Undersampling of the dominant 'nv' (Melanocytic nevi) class to 3000 samples.
Conversion of video frames to HSV colour space for real-time application.
Colour segmentation to isolate skin regions based on chromatic thresholds.
Contour detection to identify the Region of Interest (ROI) within isolated skin regions.
Cropping the identified ROI from the video frame.
Resizing the cropped ROI to 64x64 pixels for CNN input.
Normalizing the cropped ROI to ensure consistent lighting and colour distribution.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalances in the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. Minority class instances were randomly selected and duplicated to balance the class distribution.
Applied to the dominant 'nv' (Melanocytic nevi) class, reducing its sample count to 3000 to mitigate class imbalance before applying Random Over-Sampling.

conclusions: The study developed a generalized deep learning model using transfer learning with VGG16 that integrates both dermoscopic and macroscopic image datasets to enhance the scalability and accessibility of skin cancer diagnosis.
The model achieved an accuracy of 79%, an F1 score of 79%, and a Receiver Operating Characteristic Area Under the Curve (ROC AUC) score of 0.97 on the combined HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets.

The model demonstrated strong potential for real-time applications, as evidenced by its ability to classify lesions from a web-camera stream, although performance was impacted by inconsistent lighting and varying camera focal points.

The model classified vascular lesions and dermatofibroma with the highest precision, while cancerous lesions (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) achieved F1 scores of 0.72, 0.68, and 0.76, respectively.
The model exhibited confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma/Actinic Keratosis (akiec) classes, and between Melanocytic Nevi (nv) and Melanoma (mel) classes.

The research addresses the limitation of single-image diagnosis by leveraging complementary information from both dermoscopic and macroscopic perspectives, making AI-driven diagnosis more applicable in settings lacking specialized dermatoscopic equipment.
important_remarks: The study is published in a conference proceeding (CoDIT 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may limit the depth of methodological scrutiny compared to journal publications.

The model was trained on combined HAM10000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets using Random Over-Sampling and undersampling of the dominant 'nv' class to 3000 samples; this aggressive rebalancing may not reflect real-world class prevalence and could inflate performance metrics.

The input resolution was reduced to 64x64 pixels, which is significantly lower than standard dermatological analysis resolutions, potentially discarding critical fine-grained features necessary for accurate diagnosis.
The base VGG16 model was frozen (non-trainable) during fine-tuning, limiting the model's ability to adapt low-level feature extractors to the specific domain of skin lesions.

Real-world validation was limited to a preliminary web-camera stream test; performance was negatively impacted by inconsistent lighting and varying camera focal points, indicating significant sensitivity to environmental conditions not present in controlled datasets.

The paper does not report an independent external validation set; the model was evaluated on the same combined dataset used for training (with an 80:20 split), raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific distribution of these two datasets.

The model exhibited notable confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (bcc) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma/Actinic Keratosis (akiec), as well as between Melanocytic Nevi (nv) and Melanoma (mel), which are clinically critical distinctions for treatment planning.

The study lacks statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals for the reported metrics (Accuracy 79%, F1 79%, ROC AUC 0.97), making it difficult to assess the statistical significance or robustness of the results.

The real-time application component relies on OpenCV for skin detection and contouring, which introduces a dependency on accurate segmentation that can fail under poor lighting or complex backgrounds, potentially causing false negatives.

The paper acknowledges that the model is 'heavy-weighted' and suggests future work on FPGA deployment, indicating current computational inefficiencies for widespread clinical deployment on standard mobile devices.
